bytes.topic: IPFS — content addressing, DHT, pinning, IPFS vs Filecoin, CIDs
bytes.title: viem — Integrating IPFS for Decentralized Content Storage
bytes.body: When building Web3 applications with viem, content addressing via IPFS enables decentralized and persistent data storage. viem's API allows for seamless integration with IPFS, utilizing content identifiers (CIDs) to address and retrieve content. The InterPlanetary File System (IPFS) uses a distributed hash table (DHT) for efficient content discovery. To ensure content availability, pinning is crucial. While IPFS focuses on content storage, Filecoin is designed for decentralized storage networking. In viem, IPFS can be integrated using the `ipfs-http-client` library, enabling developers to create, manage, and retrieve IPFS content.

bytes.topic: The Graph — subgraphs, indexing blockchain events, GraphQL queries, hosted vs decentralized
bytes.title: Viem: Unlocking Blockchain Events with The Graph and Subgraphs
bytes.body: The Graph is a powerful tool for indexing blockchain events, enabling efficient querying via GraphQL. In viem, you can leverage The Graph's hosted service or run a decentralized node. To get started, define a subgraph using the Graph Protocol's schema language, then deploy it to a hosted service like The Graph Hosted Service or a decentralized network. In viem, use the `@graphprotocol/graph-cli` package to scaffold and deploy your subgraph. Once deployed, query your subgraph using GraphQL, taking advantage of viem's built-in support for GraphQL queries.

bytes.topic: wagmi — React hooks for Ethereum, connector system, TanStack Query integration
bytes.title: viem: Seamlessly Integrating wagmi with TanStack Query

bytes.topic: viem — TypeScript-first, lightweight, low-level, replacing ethers in modern stacks
bytes.title: viem — Replacing Ethers in Modern Blockchain Stacks
bytes.body: When building web3 applications, developers often rely on Ethers. However, viem provides a more lightweight and TypeScript-first alternative. viem's `Provider` API enables developers to connect to blockchain networks, while its `Transaction` API simplifies transaction management. For instance, to send a transaction using viem, you can use the `sendTransaction` method: `const tx = provider.getTransaction('0x...'); const receipt = await provider.sendTransaction(tx);`. This approach reduces boilerplate code and improves developer experience.
